Operating a Cisco Catalyst 8000 (Cat8k) series device is a bit of a hybrid experience. While it’s technically part of the "Catalyst" family, these are primarily Edge Platforms (Routers) rather than traditional layer-2 switches. They run Cisco IOS XE, so if you’ve used an ISR or an ASR, you'll feel right at home.

The TunTap project provides kernel extensions for Mac OS X that allow to create virtual network interfaces. From the operating system kernel's point of view, these interfaces behave similar to physical network adapters such as an Ethernet network interface. However, the virtual interface does not send the packets into a wire, but makes them available to programs running in the system.

While network devices provide several mechanisms such as SNMP, CLI and Syslog to collect data from a network, these mechanisms have limitations that restrict automation and scale. One limitation is the use of the pull model, where the request for data from network elements originates from the client. The pull model necessitates a significant amount of operational complexity with device discovery, security, and statefulness. With this model, the server sends data only when clients request it as this incurs significant overhead.
Model Driven Telemetry enables collection of system operational state data at lower latencies with lower overheads than previously possible with protocols such as SNMP.

Mathematically, the keys are inverse pairs. They are like "Plus" and "Minus" or "Multiply" and "Divide." If you do something with one, only the other can undo it.

Let me create a random secret
                  and encrypt it with that public key.
Anything the private key signs, the public key can verify

One Sentence SummaryYour Mac proves it owns the private key by solving a challenge that only the private key can solve — without ever revealing the private key itself. GitHub verifies the answer using your public key. No password needed. No secret transmitted. Mathematically unbreakable.This same principle secures your bank, your HTTPS websites, and every SSH connection in every data centre in the world — including the Huawei servers you operated.
This is not an exaggeration. It is the current mathematical consensus.

The two keys are generated from an extremely large mathematical problem called elliptic curve cryptography (that's what ed25519 means — it's the name of the specific curve used).The numbers involved are so large that:

You own a unique padlock (public key)
You own the only key that opens it (private key)

You give GitHub a copy of your padlock
GitHub keeps it on your account

When you connect:
  → GitHub locks a random secret message with YOUR padlock
  → Sends it to your Mac
  → Your Mac unlocks it with your private key
  → Sends the answer back to GitHub
  → GitHub confirms: "only the real private key could open that"
  → You are authenticated

The Analogy — Padlock and KeyImagine a special padlock system:You own a unique padlock (public key)
You own the only key that opens it (private key)

You give GitHub a copy of your padlock
GitHub keeps it on your account

When you connect:
  → GitHub locks a random secret message with YOUR padlock
  → Sends it to your Mac
  → Your Mac unlocks it with your private key
  → Sends the answer back to GitHub
  → GitHub confirms: "only the real private key could open that"
  → You are authenticated
Two keys are generated together using mathematics. What one key encrypts, only the other key can decrypt. And you cannot calculate one key from the other.

Here's the full breakdown across all four tabs. A few things worth highlighting:Why BriefGenius (Idea 02)? It wins on three dimensions that matter for a $500 / 30-day constraint: the pain is urgent (a bad brief causes immediate, visible damage to a project), the output is shareable (every PDF the client receives is a free ad), and the willingness-to-pay is high because it directly protects freelancers' income. Repurpose-Bot and RankDraft are good, but they face stronger existing competition from tools like Taplio and Surfer SEO.The key insight on the tech stack: Don't build the app in Bubble first. Build a "Zapier-tier" version in Make.com that chains Whisper → Claude → a Google Doc output in a single scenario. Let your 5 beta testers use that janky version. Only invest in a proper Bubble frontend once they're asking for more.On the marketing plan: Week 3's Product Hunt launch only works if Week 2's beta users are genuinely happy — they're your launch-day upvote army. If the beta goes poorly, delay the PH launch by a week rather than burning it with lukewarm reception.The $500 budget allocation:• Domain + Bubble starter: ~$50• Make.com + PDFMonkey: ~$20• API credits buffer (Claude + Whisper for beta): ~$30• Rewardful (referral, free until $200 MRR): $0• Reserve ~$400 for Month 2 costs while you grow to break-evenWant me to go deeper on any tab — for example, write the actual system prompt for the Claude API call, or draft the Product Hunt listing copy?

But: "I understand why layers exist — to isolate change. If the database changes, only the repository layer changes. If the API contract changes, only the controller changes. The business logic in the service layer is untouched. In our Next.js project, lib/ plays the role of the service layer for exactly that reason — if we swap net-snmp for a different SNMP library tomorrow, only lib/snmp/poller.ts changes. Nothing else in the codebase knows or cares."

The business logic in the service layer is untouched. In our Next.js project, lib/ plays the role of the service layer for exactly that reason — if we swap net-snmp for a different SNMP library tomorrow, only lib/snmp/poller.ts changes. Nothing else in the codebase knows or cares."
A junior developer who doesn't know why those decisions were made will slowly collapse all those layers into one giant route.ts file. Six months later the codebase is a mess.
The problem is: how do you stop a codebase from becoming an unmaintainable mess as it grows

The one difference is that Spring Boot enforces this separation through its dependency injection system. In Next.js you enforce it yourself through discipline — nothing stops you from putting database calls directly in a React component, but you shouldn't.

SnmpPoll and NetconfSession as separate audit tables. Every protocol interaction is persisted. This serves two purposes: the Dashboard can render historical trends (not just current state), and the Alarm Engine can correlate poll failures over time to distinguish a brief blip from a genuine outage.

Alarm is decoupled from Device via a foreign key, not embedded JSON. This lets you query alarms independently, paginate them, aggregate by severity, and index by createdAt — all impossible if alarms were a JSONB column on the device row.

System Design: Why This Schema Supports ScalabilityBefore the code, understand the three design decisions baked into this schema:1. Polymorphic Device table over separate Switch/Router tables. A deviceType enum column avoids JOIN complexity and lets you add new device classes (Firewall, AP) without migrations that alter existing tables. The tradeoff (slightly looser schema for type-specific fields) is acceptable at MVP scale and solved later with Prisma's @relation discriminated unions.
